modules.title: Hashes, Integrity, and MAC Boundaries
modules.description: Build a small integrity-checking workflow for files and messages, then compare plain hashes, keyed hashes, and HMAC in practice. The hands-on outcome is a lab that demonstrates why raw hashes do not provide authenticity, where MAC misuse happens, and how to choose between checksum, hash, HMAC, and password-hashing or KDF-style tools for different threat models.

modules.title: Authenticated Symmetric Encryption
modules.description: Implement and test safe secret-key encryption using a modern AEAD library, then analyze broken patterns like ECB, unauthenticated CBC, nonce reuse, and ad hoc encrypt-then-hash designs. The deliverable is a working encrypt/decrypt tool with tamper detection plus a misuse analysis exercise that explains what fails under modification, replay, or bad nonce handling.

modules.title: Asymmetric Primitives and Hybrid Encryption
modules.description: Build a small hybrid encryption flow that uses public-key cryptography only for key exchange or key wrapping and symmetric crypto for payload encryption. The concrete exercise is to compare RSA-style and modern key-agreement-based designs, inspect key formats, and produce a working message or file encryption demo that makes the division of responsibilities between public-key and symmetric cryptography explicit.

modules.title: Digital Signatures and Trust Decisions
modules.description: Create a signing and verification workflow for code, artifacts, or documents, then explore how signature validity differs from trustworthiness and authorization. The hands-on outcome is a mini-system that signs structured data, verifies signatures, handles key rotation or revocation scenarios, and analyzes weak designs such as treating encryption as trust or treating any signature in a keyring as approval.

modules.title: TLS Certificate Chains and Validation
modules.description: Inspect and validate real TLS certificate chains for a local or test service, tracing how hostname verification, SANs, EKUs, intermediates, and trust stores affect the outcome. The concrete lab is to diagnose certificate success and failure cases such as hostname mismatch, missing intermediates, expired certificates, and incorrect trust anchors, so the learner can reason through how X.509 works in practice.

modules.title: Issuing Certificates in a Small Internal PKI
modules.description: Create a root CA, an intermediate CA, and leaf certificates for internal services, then issue and deploy certificates with constrained purposes and lifetimes. The deliverable is a private PKI lab that covers chain construction, internal trust distribution, revocation and rotation concerns, and the risks of overpowered internal CAs or misissued certificates.

modules.title: SSH Keys, Host Verification, and SSH Certificates
modules.description: Set up practical SSH authentication workflows with agents, passphrase-protected keys, and `known_hosts`, then examine how host identity is established and how SSH certificates change operational trust. The concrete lab is to connect to local or test hosts, inspect and manage host keys, reproduce host verification events, and issue short-lived SSH user or host certificates to understand the difference between raw keys and CA-backed SSH trust.

modules.title: PGP Encryption, Signing, and Key Trust
modules.description: Perform real PGP file or email-style signing and encryption tasks, then evaluate key distribution, fingerprint verification, subkeys, revocation, and trust assignment. The hands-on outcome is a working OpenPGP workflow plus a trust-management exercise that forces the learner to reason about identity binding, key rollover, lost private keys, and why PGP trust semantics are harder than the crypto primitives themselves.
